steps: Recall that \(a^{p/q} = \sqrt[q]{a^p}\). Here the exponent is \(\frac{11}{12}\), so \(a^{11/12} = \sqrt[12]{a^{11}}\).

To match the form \(\sqrt[n]{a^{132}}\) used in the answer choices, scale the exponent so the numerator becomes 132. Multiply numerator and denominator by 12: $$\frac{11}{12} = \frac{11 \times 12}{12 \times 12} = \frac{132}{144}$$ so \(a^{11/12} = a^{132/144} = \sqrt[144]{a^{132}}\).

Evaluate each choice:
  • A: \(\sqrt[12]{a^{132}} = a^{132/12} = a^{11}\). This equals \(a^{11}\), not \(a^{11/12}\). Eliminated.
  • B: \(\sqrt[144]{a^{132}} = a^{132/144} = a^{11/12}\). This equals the given expression exactly. That matches choice B — B is correct.
  • C: \(\sqrt[121]{a^{132}} = a^{132/121} = a^{12/11}\). The exponent \(12/11 \neq 11/12\). Eliminated.
  • D: \(\sqrt[11]{a^{132}} = a^{132/11} = a^{12}\). This equals \(a^{12}\), not \(a^{11/12}\). Eliminated.
